title: The Body That Drives: Merleau-Ponty’s Phenomenology of Embodiment in Baby Driver
author: Harrison McLain
abstract: Harrison McLain examines Edgar Wright’s Baby Driver through Maurice Merleau-Ponty’s phenomenology of embodiment, arguing that the film enacts key claims about the body-schema, the intentional arc, and sedimented habit—making it one of the most philosophically precise films in recent cinema.

title: Beyond the Diss Track: A Sociological Analysis of Kendrick Lamar’s Not Like Us
author: Luke Marshall
abstract: This article examines Kendrick Lamar’s 2024 song Not Like Us through a sociological lens. By analyzing themes of social stratification, social mobility, race, ethnicity, cultural appropriation, and colonialism, the article explores how contemporary hip-hop can serve as a form of social commentary. Rather than focusing on the celebrity dispute itself, the analysis demonstrates how popular music reflects broader debates surrounding identity, authenticity, power, and cultural ownership.

title: Revenue Recognition in the Airline Industry: A Case Study of Delta Air Lines
author: Luke Marshall
abstract: This finance case study examines how Delta Air Lines applies ASC 606 to passenger ticket sales, its SkyMiles loyalty program, and ancillary services, and what those accounting choices mean for investors.

title: When Goodwill Disappears: Lessons from Coty's $3.9 Billion Impairment
author: Luke Marshall
abstract: This finance case study examines Coty Inc.'s 2019 goodwill impairment following its acquisition of Procter & Gamble's beauty brands.
